topic: Daytona Review 2026: Sub-90ms AI Agent Code Execution Infrastructure

key_points: microVM-based isolation (Firecracker), dedicated kernel per session
~150ms cold start — slower than Daytona's sub-90ms
$0.0504/vCPU-hour, same as Daytona; $150/month Pro tier
24-hour max session duration (vs Daytona's unlimited)
Large catalog of community sandbox templates
Strong Python iteration use case and Firecracker-grade security
gVisor-based isolation; sub-second cold starts
Only platform offering GPU-accelerated sandboxes (~3x CPU cost)
Unlimited session duration
Best fit for inference, fine-tuning, or image processing inside sandboxes
More expensive than Daytona for pure CPU workloads
Container-based sandbox runner with unlimited session duration
Publishes detailed 2026 comparison blogs benchmarking Daytona vs E2B vs Modal
Focuses on platform engineering and DevOps use cases
New entrant to the AI code execution sandbox space in 2026
Tightly integrated with Vercel deployment platform
Primarily targets frontend/full-stack Next.js agent workflows
AgentBox: alternative sandbox runtime for AI agents
DIY Docker approach: free but 2-second cold start per sandbox operation
DIY math: 15 tool calls x 2s = 30s overhead vs Daytona's ~1.3s total

stats.fact: Daytona sandboxes spin up in under 90ms; optimized configurations reach 27ms cold starts
Daytona raised a $24M Series A in February 2026 led by FirstMark Capital, with participation from Pace Capital, Upfront Ventures, Darkmode, E2VC, Datadog, and Figma Ventures
Daytona reached a $1M forward revenue run rate in under three months after launch; doubled six weeks later
Pricing: $0.0504/vCPU-hour and $0.0162/GiB-hour, billed per second; $200 free credits at signup; startup credits up to $50,000 available
E2B cold start is ~150ms vs Daytona's sub-90ms; Modal is sub-second for GPU sandboxes
Docker DIY approach: 2-second overhead per sandbox operation; across 15 agent tool calls = 30s total vs Daytona's ~1.3s
Customers include LangChain, Turing, Writer, SambaNova, Orchids, and Laude Institute (partnered with Stanford for Terminal Bench)
Sandboxes auto-archive after 30 days of continuous stopped state (configurable via auto_archive_interval)
Daytona is open source under the GNU AFFERO GENERAL PUBLIC LICENSE (AGPL)
Laude Institute's Terminal Bench (with Stanford) runs thousands of parallel AI agent experiments simultaneously using Daytona

angles: Speed wins agentic AI: at 15 tool calls per task, Docker's 30s overhead vs Daytona's 1.3s is a night-and-day developer experience difference
Open-source AGPL license means no vendor lock-in — self-host on your own infra if data sovereignty is required
Series A momentum: $24M led by FirstMark + Datadog/Figma Ventures signals enterprise-grade trust in the platform
'Give every agent a computer' — Daytona's vision of agent-native infrastructure vs. repurposed human-centric cloud primitives
Real customer proof: SambaNova, LangChain, Orchids (world's first AI full-stack engineer), and Stanford benchmarks give credibility
Pricing simplicity: no monthly base fee, pay-per-second billing beats E2B's $150/month Pro tier for lighter users
The GPU gap: Daytona beats E2B on speed and open-source, but Modal wins if you need GPU inside sandboxes
Computer Use API unlocks agent-driven browser automation, GUI testing, and desktop simulation beyond simple code exec
